quote: I assume you do you have AEP or AES?
stewart_read: Parker skips the interest_in_solar verify item entirely — the schema's highest-weighted emotional anchor — and jumps directly to utility company. The verify section's coaching note is explicit: 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR. Depth matters more than the answer. Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Mildred had already signaled bill pain ('I thought that was probably a joke' about the no-cost claim) and would have been receptive to this question. The skip is reactive — Parker is moving through logistics rather than building emotional alignment.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read Mildred's 'what are they wanting to do?' as a signal to explain the program first and circle back to motivation — a strategic sequencing choice. But there is no evidence of a later return to this question anywhere in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the verify.interest_in_solar coaching note. The question 'What interested you in solar?' at 00:37 would have surfaced Mildred's bill pain organically and set up the bill flip at 02:35. The manager should ask Parker: 'Where in this call did you find out why Mildred wanted solar?' The answer is: never.

quote: it's a townhouse, and it's in a condominium association.
stewart_read: Parker never asks who else is on the title. Mildred is 84, on a walker, has a helper who comes one day a week, and her granddaughter is visiting from Florida. The adult_child_decision_maker protocol is potentially applicable — if a family member is on the title or functionally co-decides, the appointment is structurally vulnerable without them present. Parker's HOA team mention (02:00) addresses the association question but the title/co-owners item is never touched.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk Parker through protocols.adult_child_decision_maker. At minimum, Parker should have asked 'Is there anyone else on the title with you?' before booking. If a family member is a co-decider, the closer walks into a one-person appointment that can't close.

quote: They have a what now? I can't hear good, honey. And I've got my hearing aids in, but I think the batteries are dead.
stewart_read: Mildred discloses a hearing impairment mid-call — batteries dead in her hearing aids. Parker's response is 'Oh, no worries' and then repeats the credit question more slowly. The communication accommodation is adequate, but the disclosure itself — an elderly woman alone, hearing aids failing, about to lie down — is a trust gift that receives no acknowledgment. This is the first of three vulnerability disclosures that go unacknowledged across the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that dwelling on the hearing issue would slow the call and embarrass Mildred. A strategic read would say: move past it efficiently and keep momentum. But the schema is explicit that vulnerability disclosures are trust gifts, not interruptions — and the pattern repeats twice more, making the strategic read untenable.
coaching_implication: The manager should use this moment to introduce coaching_philosophy.empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure. A single sentence — 'I'm sorry, let me slow down for you' — costs nothing and builds the trust layer the closer will need. The manager should ask Parker: 'What did you say when she told you her hearing aids were dead?' and let Parker hear the gap.

quote: I'm so tired, and I think I'm gonna fall down. I was getting ready to lay down when you called.
stewart_read: This is the second vulnerability disclosure — physical exhaustion, near-collapse, interrupted rest. Parker's response is to pivot immediately to scheduling availability: 'I know we have some availability as early as, like, tomorrow or Saturday.' The disclosure is treated as a scheduling constraint rather than a human moment. Per the schema, the correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value — not skip to logistics.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read Mildred's mention of wanting someone to 'come and explain it' as the actionable signal and moved to scheduling to honor her stated preference. That's a plausible reactive read. But the exhaustion disclosure came first and was more urgent — the strategic move would have been to acknowledge it before pivoting.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 03:14 moment in the 1-on-1. Show Parker the pattern: two vulnerability disclosures in 60 seconds, both bypassed. A suggested rephrase for 04:01: 'I'm sorry to catch you at a bad time — let's make this quick and get you set up so you can rest.' That one sentence acknowledges the disclosure and keeps momentum.

quote: Yeah. That's my address. I've been here thirty eight years, sir.
stewart_read: Thirty-eight years of tenure is a strong qualification signal — long-planned homeowner, not a flight risk, high likelihood of being present for the closer appointment. Parker says nothing about it. The schema notes that long tenure is an enthusiasm signal ('this is our retirement home' category). Parker could have anchored on this: 'Thirty-eight years — so you've been paying Duke Energy for thirty-eight years. That's a long time to be on their schedule.' That's the bill flip setup, and it was sitting right here.
coaching_implication: Show Parker that the 38-year tenure line was a bill-flip setup handed to them on a plate. The manager should ask: 'What could you have said right there that would have made Mildred feel the weight of 38 years of utility bills?' This is the coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion entry point — the flip was available and unused.

quote: Yeah. It would. Because I had one bill that was $388.
stewart_read: Mildred volunteers her peak bill unprompted and frames it as a shared grievance. Parker's response: 'That's that's really high. Yeah. Yeah. That is really what's kind of an average bill for you, would you say?' — captures the data and moves on. The bill is anchored but not flipped. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the pattern should be 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' Parker executes 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The $388 peak and $150–$180 average are sitting on the table unused as emotional leverage.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have been saving the bill flip for the in-person appointment, where the closer can run the numbers. That's a plausible strategic read for a setter who knows the closer will do the financial presentation. But the flip doesn't require numbers — it requires emotion. 'Thirty-eight years of Duke Energy bills, and the highest one was $388. That's what we're replacing.' No fabricated numbers needed.
coaching_implication: This is the universal coaching priority across the corpus. The manager should use this moment to introduce coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion with a concrete example: 'What could Parker have said right after Mildred said $388 that would have made her feel that number differently?' The answer doesn't require a savings figure — it requires redirecting the pain of the number toward the utility, not toward solar.

quote: I'm getting some flooring put in and some other stuff done to the condo. So, they'll be coming, for that
stewart_read: Mildred is actively investing in her home — flooring, windows, doors, insulation, a stairlift, carpet on the stairs. This is a long-tenure homeowner in active improvement mode, not a passive resident. The energy-efficiency investments (insulation, new windows) are adjacent to solar's value proposition and could have been used to reinforce appointment stickiness: 'You're already thinking about making the home more efficient — solar fits right into that.' The schema does not yet have a cross-sell section for energy-efficiency adjacent services, but the engagement signal is real and unused.
coaching_implication: Parker responded with 'Nice. Nice.' — a filler that signals the rep heard the words but not the signal. The manager should ask Parker: 'What did Mildred's home improvement list tell you about her as a customer?' The answer — active investor, long-tenure, efficiency-minded — is the profile that makes this appointment worth protecting. Use this to reinforce appointment stickiness framing: the closer should know Mildred is already in improvement mode.

stewart_read: The appointment is booked at 5PM Saturday, but none of the button_up required elements are executed: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promised. The booking exists as a verbal agreement between two people — one of whom is 84, exhausted, and has a full Saturday. The conditional nature is compounded by the uncollected bill and the Friday callback dependency.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that running through the full button-up protocol with an 84-year-old who was about to fall down would have been counterproductive — better to confirm the time and let her rest. That's a reasonable read on energy management. But the 'is that fair' handshake and the text follow-up promise are low-friction and high-value for show rate — skipping them entirely is a gap, not a strategic choice.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through button_up required elements one by one and ask which ones were completed on this call. The answer is zero. The manager should focus on the two lowest-friction elements Parker could have added: the 'is that fair' handshake and the text follow-up promise. Both take under 10 seconds and materially affect show rate.
